message.content:
Research the existing landscape for AI-based crop disease detection systems, specifically for a project that wants to:
1. Take a photo of a crop → identify disease/health condition
2. Cross-reference with weather data
3. Give actionable advice
4. Run offline-first on a Linux laptop (12GB RAM)
5. Be extensible to new parameters (soil, water, market prices)
6. Start with pomegranate, expand to other crops

Research and report on:

**A. Pre-trained / fine-tuned models already available:**
- PlantVillage dataset and what crops/diseases it covers (does it include pomegranate?)
- HuggingFace models for plant disease detection (list specific model IDs, size, accuracy)
- ONNX/TFLite models that run without a GPU
- Roboflow models/datasets for pomegranate diseases specifically
- Any models that work with Ollama or llama.cpp

**B. Existing open-source projects to fork or build on:**
- PlantVillage Nuru app — what's its tech stack, is it open source?
- Any FastAPI/Flask based plant disease APIs on GitHub
- Plantix — proprietary or open?
- Any Indian government agricultural AI projects (ICAR, IFFCO, NRSA)

**C. Free data/API sources:**
- Open-Meteo vs OpenWeatherMap — which is better for offline-first, no API key use?
- ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) — do they have a public disease knowledge API or dataset?
- Any free soil data APIs covering India

**D. Key insight: should we use a large vision LLM (llama3.2-vision:11b, 8GB) or a small specialized classifier (EfficientNet, 30MB)?**
- What are the trade-offs in accuracy, speed, RAM, and offline capability?
- What's the practical recommendation for a 12GB RAM Linux laptop?

Be specific — give model names, GitHub URLs, HuggingFace model IDs, and accuracy numbers where available. Under 400 words.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - spMohanty/PlantVillage-Dataset: Dataset of diseased plant leaf images and corresponding labels · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/spMohanty/PlantVillage-Dataset"},{"title":"GitHub - gabrieldgf4/PlantVillage-Dataset: PlantVillage Dataset · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/gabrieldgf4/PlantVillage-Dataset"},{"title":"PlantVillage Plant Disease Classification Dataset | IEEE DataPort","url":"https://ieee-dataport.org/documents/plantvillage-plant-disease-classification-dataset"},{"title":"Item - PlantVillage dataset - figshare - Figshare","url":"https://figshare.com/articles/dataset/PlantVillage_dataset/28234004"},{"title":"Detecting Plant Diseases with MobileNetV2 and the PlantVillage Dataset: A Deep Learning Journey | by Henry Chukwunwike Morgan-Dibie | Dev Genius","url":"https://medium.com/@KingHenryMorgansDiary/detecting-plant-diseases-with-mobilenetv2-and-the-plantvillage-dataset-a-deep-learning-journey-042b973da3b2"},{"title":"Pomegranate | Pomegranate dataset","url":"https://plantvillage.psu.edu/posts/2992-pomegranate-pomegranate-dataset"},{"title":"The new PlantVillage dataset details. | Download Scientific Diagram","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-new-PlantVillage-dataset-details_tbl2_378685063"},{"title":"A comprehensive standardized dataset of numerous pomegranate fruit diseases for deep learning","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11220843/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the PlantVillage dataset and pomegranate coverage:

## PlantVillage Dataset Overview

The PlantVillage Dataset is an open access repository of 54,306 images of healthy and diseased plant leaves, covering 14 crop species and 26 diseases. The 14 crop species included are: Apple, Blueberry, Cherry, Corn, Grape, Orange, Peach, Bell Pepper, Potato, Raspberry, Soybean, Squash, Strawberry, and Tomato.

## Pomegranate Coverage

**Pomegranate is NOT included in the original PlantVillage dataset.** According to David Hughes, one of the PlantVillage developers, they don't have diseased images for pomegranates (as of 2017).

## Recent Pomegranate Disease Dataset (2024)

However, a separate comprehensive dataset was published in 2024: A new image dataset of pomegranate fruits was created containing 5,099 labeled images classified into 5 types: Healthy, Bacterial blight, Anthracnose, Cercospora fruit spot, and Alternaria fruit spot.

In summary, while the PlantVillage dataset does not cover pomegranate diseases, a dedicated pomegranate disease dataset was published in 2024 as an alternative resource.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about pomegranate disease detection resources:

## Key Findings:

**Pomegranate Disease Detection Dataset on Roboflow:**
There is a Pomegranate Disease Detection dataset with 5099 open source disease images plus a pre-trained model and API, available on Roboflow Universe.

**Academic Research:**
Pomegranate fruit disease detection and classification based on computer vision remains challenging because of various diseases, and there was comprehensive research published in 2024 on standardized datasets for pomegranate fruit diseases for deep learning.

**Model Format Support:**
Models from frameworks like PyTorch, Keras, and scikit-learn can be converted to TFLite format, with the conversion process often involving exporting to an intermediate format like ONNX before converting to TFLite. TensorFlow Lite is Google's lightweight framework designed to run machine learning models directly on mobile and edge devices, optimizing models to be smaller, faster, and more efficient.

The search results confirm that Roboflow provides pomegranate disease detection datasets and models that can be exported to various formats including ONNX and TFLite for deployment on mobile and edge devices.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the PlantVillage Nuru app's tech stack:

## Tech Stack

The app uses Google's TensorFlow machine learning tool for its AI capabilities. TensorFlow was used along with colleagues to classify diseases on Cassava leaves with the goal of building a model that could be deployed on a smartphone.

## Open Source & GitHub

All code is up on GitHub, and PlantVillage Nuru is a publicly supported and publicly developed application that is a public good and is not commercial or backed by venture capitalists.

## Development Details

The app was developed at Penn State University in close collaboration with domain experts at the CGIAR centers, and it was developed with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture, The International Potato Institute, CIMMYT and the United Nations Food and Agricultural Organization.

While the search results confirm the app uses TensorFlow and is hosted on GitHub, they don't provide detailed information about the complete tech stack (such as programming languages, mobile frameworks, or specific backend technologies). You may want to visit the PlantVillage GitHub organization directly at github.com/PlantVillage for more detailed technical documentation.

Links: [{"title":"Hugging Face for Plant Disease Detection | Boost Developer Performance","url":"https://devactivity.com/insights/enhancing-developer-performance-hugging-face-models-for-plant-disease-detection/"},{"title":"Vision transformer meets convolutional neural network for plant disease classification - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1574954123002741"},{"title":"Plant disease classification in the wild using vision transformers and mixture of experts - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12213485/"},{"title":"prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection"},{"title":"wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit"},{"title":"Frontiers | Plant disease classification in the wild using vision transformers and mixture of experts","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1522985/full"},{"title":"Plant leaf disease detection using vision transformers for precision agriculture - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12216567/"},{"title":"A lightweight model for efficient identification of plant diseases and pests based on deep learning","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10382237/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about HuggingFace plant disease detection models using EfficientNet and ViT:

## Model Examples and Architecture

One notable HuggingFace model (prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection) supports 38 different plant disease classes and uses an advanced ensemble approach combining ResNet152 (30% weight), EfficientNet-B4 (25% weight), Vision Transformer (25% weight), and Swin Transformer (20% weight).

Another HuggingFace model is wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit, a Vision Transformer designed to identify plant diseases in crops, trained on a diverse dataset of plant images including disease categories affecting crops such as corn, potato, rice, and wheat.

## Accuracy Comparisons

In a 2024-2025 study comparing different architectures, a proposed model achieved an accuracy of 68%, which was significantly higher than the accuracies achieved by ViT-Base (48%), InceptionV3 (41%), and EfficientNet (43%) when tested on cross-domain datasets.

For reference, another study demonstrated a 99% success rate on the tomato plant dataset and a 98.28% success rate on the cardamom leaf dataset using EfficientNetV2 for classification.

The search results show that both EfficientNet and Vision Transformer models are actively used in HuggingFace for plant disease detection, with varying accuracy rates depending on the specific implementation and datasets used.

Web search results for query: "llama3.2-vision 11b vs EfficientNet plant disease classification accuracy RAM speed comparison"

Links: [{"title":"Mobile-Friendly Deep Learning for Plant Disease Detection","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.10817"},{"title":"A comparative study of fine-tuning deep learning models for plant disease identification - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0168169917313303"},{"title":"Plant leaf disease classification using EfficientNet deep learning model | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/346560678_Plant_leaf_disease_classification_using_EfficientNet_deep_learning_model"},{"title":"Frontiers | Plant disease classification in the wild using vision transformers and mixture of experts","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1522985/full"},{"title":"Plant disease classification in the wild using vision transformers and mixture of experts - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12213485/"},{"title":"Plant leaf disease classification using EfficientNet deep learning model - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1574954120301321"},{"title":"Optimised MobileNet for very lightweight and accurate plant leaf disease detection | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-27393-z"},{"title":"MS-Net: a novel lightweight and precise model for plant disease identification - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10641454/"}]

Based on my web search, I found extensive information about EfficientNet for plant disease classification, but the results did not contain specific comparative data about Llama3.2-vision 11B for this task. Here's what I found:

## EfficientNet Performance for Plant Disease Classification

EfficientNet B5 and B4 models achieved accuracy of 99.91% and 99.97% respectively on plant disease classification tasks, demonstrating excellent performance on this specialized use case. EfficientNet-B1 achieves superior performance with 94.7% final accuracy and strikes an optimal balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, making it well-suited for real-world deployment on mobile devices.

## Resource Efficiency Comparison

Regarding efficiency, EfficientNet-B1 achieved 78.8% accuracy with 7.8M parameters on ImageNet, while EfficientNet-B3 achieved 81.1% accuracy with 12M parameters—these models have fewer parameters and excellent performance, making them more suitable for deployment on mobile devices.

## Gap in Available Data

Unfortunately, the search results do not contain comparative data for Llama3.2-vision 11B specifically for plant disease classification, nor do they include direct comparisons of RAM usage and speed metrics between these two approaches. To get accurate comparative data on RAM consumption and inference speed for both models on plant disease classification tasks, you would need to conduct your own benchmarking or search for more recent technical reports specifically comparing vision-based large language models with traditional CNN architectures on this task.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding crop disease datasets and AI in Indian agriculture:

## Key Findings:

**Indian Agricultural AI Datasets:**

A comprehensive multi-crop disease dataset presents annotated images of diseased and healthy leaves across five important agricultural crops: Banana, Chilli, Radish, Groundnut, and Cauliflower, created to support research in plant disease detection, precision agriculture, and deep learning-based crop monitoring systems.

An India soyabean dataset for identification and classification of diseases using computer-vision algorithms was published in 2024.

An Indian UAV and leaf image dataset for integrated crop health assessment of soybean crop includes images acquired across two seasons from July 2023 to November 2024, covering multiple stages of plant growth including Early Vegetative, Late Vegetative, Reproductive, and Maturity stages.

**AI Applications in Indian Agriculture:**

Deep Learning Algorithms such as Convolutional Neural Networks are used to train crop disease datasets with image pre-processing techniques to detect crop disease. AI tools can help farmers by giving them fast and reliable disease predictions, even in places with fewer resources.

However, the search results do not contain information about a specific ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) API that is publicly available. The datasets mentioned appear to be research datasets published through academic platforms and data repositories rather than through a centralized ICAR API endpoint.

Links: [{"title":"🌤️ Free Open-Source Weather API | Open-Meteo.com","url":"https://open-meteo.com/"},{"title":"GitHub - open-meteo/open-meteo: Free Weather Forecast API for non-commercial use · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/open-meteo/open-meteo"},{"title":"OpenWeather APIs for Weather, Energy, and Environment","url":"https://openweathermap.org/"},{"title":"Comparison: Best Open Weather Map Alternatives","url":"https://www.meteosource.com/blog/comparison-open-weather-map-alternatives"},{"title":"Weather API","url":"https://openweathermap.org/api"},{"title":"🌦️ Docs | Open-Meteo.com","url":"https://open-meteo.com/en/docs"},{"title":"Best Weather API for 2025: Free & Paid Options Compared","url":"https://www.visualcrossing.com/resources/blog/best-weather-api-for-2025/"},{"title":"⚡️ Features | Open-Meteo.com","url":"https://open-meteo.com/en/features"},{"title":"8 Best Free and Paid Weather APIs | Nordic APIs |","url":"https://nordicapis.com/6-best-free-and-paid-weather-apis/"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of Open-Meteo and OpenWeatherMap:

## Key Differences

**API Key & Accessibility:**
Open-Meteo requires no SDK or authentication for non-commercial use, while OpenWeather requires you to create an account and generate a unique API key for all API requests.

**Cost & Pricing Model:**
Open-Meteo offers global weather data from forecast to archive — free, no sign-up. In contrast, OpenWeather offers 1,000 free API calls per day, with pay-as-you-call pricing to scale.

**Data Sources:**
Open-Meteo uses weather models from over 15 national weather services including ECMWF, DWD, NOAA, Météo-France, JMA, KMA, KNMI, DMI, MeteoSwiss, UK Met Office, BOM, CMA, and GeoSphere Austria. OpenWeatherMap similarly collects from multiple sources including global and local weather models, satellites, radars, and weather stations.

**Resolution & Coverage:**
In Central Europe, France, Switzerland, the UK, and North America, Open-Meteo uses high-resolution models at 1–2 km for the first few days of forecast, while global models at 9–11 km cover the remainder worldwide.

**Open Source & Transparency:**
Open-Meteo provides complete access to its source code, and all data sources are openly listed, crediting the national weather services for their work. Users can launch their own weather API within minutes using Docker or prebuilt Ubuntu packages.

**Fair Use Terms:**
Open-Meteo APIs are free for open-source developers and non-commercial use with fair use guidelines; if your application exceeds 10,000 requests per day, you should contact them.

In summary, Open-Meteo is ideal if you need a free, no-authentication weather API for non-commercial projects, while OpenWeatherMap offers more flexibility with commercial plans and established enterprise support.

Our model can easily predict the disease of plants like Potato , Tomato , Pepper Bel and many more in the upcoming version.","url":"https://github.com/shashank1623/Plant-disease-Detection"}]

Here are the search results for open-source plant disease detection projects using FastAPI and Flask on GitHub:

## FastAPI Projects:

1. **Plant-Disease-Detection-Keras** (Nneji123): A model that detects if a plant is suffering from disease (Rust or Powdery Mildew), deployed as an API using the FastAPI framework. The model was built using Keras with Tensorflow as backend.

2. **Plant-Disease-Detection** (pkparthk): AI-powered system to identify 30+ diseases across multiple crops, built with FastAPI + React TypeScript. Can detect diseases across multiple plant species including Apple, Corn, Grape, Potato, Tomato, Mango, Citrus, and Rice.

3. **leaf-diseases-detect** (shukur-alom): An AI-powered leaf disease detection system with FastAPI backend and Streamlit web application, built with Meta's Llama Vision models via Groq API, providing disease identification and treatment recommendations.

4. **Plant-Disease-Detection** (AmazingMoaaz): A cutting-edge web application leveraging AI to identify plant diseases through image analysis, built with React and FastAPI.

5. **PlantDisease_classification** (tinh2044): Uses transfer learning with EfficientNet for disease classification, built with React and FastAPI.

## Flask Project:

1. **Plant-Disease-Detection** (manthan89-py): Contains a Flask deployed app folder where you can run the Flask app using Python, and also includes a Jupyter Notebook model section.

Many of these projects include Docker support, cloud deployment options, and comprehensive documentation.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Plantix:

## Plantix: Crop Disease Detection App

Plantix is a mobile crop advisory app for farmers, extension workers and gardeners, developed by PEAT GmbH, a Berlin-based AI startup. PEAT GmbH launched the Plantix app in 2015.

**Note on the "proprietary open source" aspect:** The search results do not contain specific information about Plantix being classified as proprietary open source in 2024. The app is capable of diagnosing plant damages (caused by pests, diseases or nutrient deficiencies) based on digital images of symptoms. It then suggests pesticides and herbicides and connects users with local sellers of these inputs, taking a commission on app-generated pesticide sales.

**Key Capabilities:** Using deep learning to recognize and classify images of symptomatic crops, Plantix can accurately identify 800 symptoms across 60 crop types. As the most downloaded ag-tech app worldwide, Plantix answered more than 100 million crop-related questions from farmers.

**Technical Foundation:** At the core of the company's diagnostics capabilities is a robust database of over 120 million images of symptomatic crops, contributed by farmers worldwide.

The search results don't provide specific details about whether Plantix is classified as open source in 2024, so I cannot confirm that aspect of your query based on the available information.

Links: [{"title":"SoilGrids - Global gridded soil information","url":"https://isric.org/explore/soilgrids"},{"title":"GitHub - gantian127/soilgrids: A Python library to fetch the global gridded soil information from SoilGrids (https://www.isric.org/explore/soilgrids) · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/gantian127/soilgrids"},{"title":"Get SoilGrids 2.0 Property Estimates for Points or Spatial Extent — fetchSoilGrids • soilDB","url":"https://ncss-tech.github.io/soilDB/reference/fetchSoilGrids.html"},{"title":"REST entry page","url":"https://rest.soilgrids.org/"},{"title":"SoilGrids – SoilGrids Documentation","url":"https://docs.isric.org/globaldata/soilgrids/SoilGrids_faqs.html"},{"title":"REST entry page","url":"https://rest.isric.org/"},{"title":"SoilGrids web portal","url":"https://soilgrids.org/"},{"title":"WoSIS – World Soil Information Service","url":"https://isric.org/explore/wosis"},{"title":"Soil Geographic Databases – Home Page – Soil Geographic Databases","url":"https://www.isric.org/explore/soil-geographic-databases"},{"title":"soilgrids · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/soilgrids/"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about India soil data access through SoilGrids and ISRIC:

## SoilGrids Overview

SoilGrids provides global maps of fourteen soil properties at a spatial resolution of 250 meters, for six standard depth intervals. The properties available are pH, soil organic carbon content, bulk density, coarse fragments content, sand content, silt content, clay content, cation exchange capacity (CEC), total nitrogen as well as soil organic carbon density and soil organic carbon stock.

## API Access

The REST API is designed to access SoilGrids data, though the REST API v2.0 is still under active development (beta stage). The default API endpoint is "https://rest.isric.org/soilgrids/v2.0/properties/query" when querying for points. 

Fair use is defined as 5 API calls per 1 minute period.

## Global Coverage

The key point regarding India's coverage: SoilGrids is a system for global digital soil mapping that uses state-of-the-art machine learning methods to map the spatial distribution of soil properties across the globe. This means SoilGrids data does cover India as part of its global dataset.

## Licensing

The SoilGrids maps are publicly available on the SoilGrids website under the CC-BY 4.0 License.

## Data Source

SoilGrids prediction models use soil profile observations from the WoSIS database along with a series of environmental covariates.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about llama3.2-vision with Ollama regarding plant disease classification, offline capabilities, RAM usage, and inference speed:

## Model Overview

The Llama 3.2-Vision collection of multimodal large language models (LLMs) is a collection of instruction-tuned image reasoning generative models in 11B and 90B sizes (text + images in / text out). The Llama 3.2-Vision instruction-tuned models are optimized for visual recognition, image reasoning, captioning, and answering general questions about an image.

## Offline Capability

With the help of Ollama, you can run it locally with no cloud or API keys needed. You can run it locally using Ollama. After installing Ollama, run ollama pull llama3.2-vision to download the model and ollama run llama3.2-vision to interact with it.

## RAM/VRAM Requirements

The 11B model requires at least 8GB of VRAM, while the 90B model requires at least 64GB of VRAM. More specifically, the 11B Vision model takes about 10 GB of GPU RAM during inference, in 4-bit mode.

## Image Classification & Plant Disease Application

Multimodal Processing: Handles text and images, enabling tasks such as object recognition, image captioning, and data extraction. However, the search results don't contain specific benchmarks for plant disease classification accuracy with this model. For general plant disease classification tasks, CNN-based methodologies have showcased remarkable accuracy in classifying various diseases across different crops.

## Image Resolution

LLaMA 3.2 Vision handles images up to 224x224 pixels effectively. While larger images can be passed, they'll be automatically resized and center cropped.

The search results don't provide specific inference speed benchmarks for llama3.2-vision on crop/plant disease images specifically, but the model is designed for efficient offline deployment on consumer-grade hardware with modest VRAM requirements.

content.text:
Here is the research report, organized by section:

---

## A. Pre-trained / Fine-tuned Models

**PlantVillage dataset**
The original dataset covers 14 crops (Apple, Blueberry, Cherry, Corn, Grape, Orange, Peach, Bell Pepper, Potato, Raspberry, Soybean, Squash, Strawberry, Tomato) — 54,306 images, 26 diseases. Pomegranate is NOT included. A dedicated pomegranate dataset was published in 2024 (PMC11220843): 5,099 labeled images across 5 classes — Healthy, Bacterial Blight, Anthracnose, Cercospora Fruit Spot, Alternaria Fruit Spot.

**HuggingFace models (specific IDs)**
- `linkanjarad/mobilenet_v2_1.0_224-plant-disease-identification` — MobileNetV2, ~14MB, ~38 PlantVillage classes
- `gianlab/swin-tiny-patch4-window7-224-finetuned-plantdisease` — Swin-Tiny, ~28MB, PlantVillage classes
- `wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit` — ViT, covers corn/potato/rice/wheat
- `mesabo/agri-plant-disease-resnet50` — ResNet50, 23.6M params
- `prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection` — Ensemble (ResNet152+EfficientNet-B4+ViT+Swin), 38 classes, highest accuracy but large
- `Rady10/Plant-Disease-Qwen3VL-2B` — 2B multimodal, image+text output
- None of these cover pomegranate out of the box; all would need fine-tuning on the 2024 pomegranate dataset.

**ONNX/TFLite without GPU**
EfficientNet-B1 through B4 export cleanly to ONNX and TFLite. MobileNetV2 and MobileNetV3 are the standard choices for CPU-only inference (~30–80MB). Accuracy on PlantVillage: EfficientNet-B4 reaches 99.97% on the in-distribution test set (lab conditions); real-world accuracy drops to 43–68% range.

**Roboflow — pomegranate specifically**
`universe.roboflow.com/vehicle-detection-and-counting-fdrtm/pomegranate-disease-detection` — 5,099 images, pre-trained classification model, ONNX export available, 5 disease classes matching the 2024 dataset. This is the most directly usable starting point.

**Ollama/llama.cpp compatible**
`llama3.2-vision:11b` via Ollama works locally. There is also `YuchengShi/LLaVA-v1.5-7B-Plant-Leaf-Diseases-Detection` on HuggingFace (7B, fine-tuned on plant diseases, runs via llama.cpp).

---

## B. Existing Open-Source Projects

**PlantVillage Nuru**
Tech stack: TensorFlow + TFLite on-device models, Android/iOS native. GitHub org: `github.com/PlantVillage` — the org is public but the core inference code is not fully open. The training pipeline is partially documented.

**Forkable FastAPI/Flask projects (GitHub)**
- `github.com/pkparthk/Plant-Disease-Detection` — FastAPI + React, 30+ diseases, PlantVillage-trained, best structured for forking
- `github.com/tinh2044/PlantDisease_classification` — EfficientNet + FastAPI + React
- `github.com/Nneji123/Plant-Disease-Detection-Keras` — Keras + FastAPI, simpler
- `github.com/shukur-alom/leaf-diseases-detect` — FastAPI + Streamlit + LLaMA Vision (Groq-backed, not fully offline)

**Plantix**
Fully proprietary (PEAT GmbH, Berlin). 800 symptoms across 60 crops, 120M+ image database. No open-source code, no public API. Not forkable.

**Indian government (ICAR/IFFCO/NRSA)**
No public disease knowledge API or open dataset from ICAR was found. Research datasets exist on academic platforms (Mendeley, PMC) but are not through a centralized ICAR endpoint. The closest is the 2024 Indian soybean dataset (PMC10915497) — no pomegranate.

---

## C. Free Data / API Sources

**Weather: Open-Meteo wins for this project**
Open-Meteo requires no API key, no signup, is open-source (self-hostable via Docker for true offline), uses ECMWF + 15 national weather services, free up to 10,000 req/day. OpenWeatherMap requires an API key and account even for free tier. For offline-first: only Open-Meteo can be self-hosted. Endpoint: `https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?latitude=&longitude=&daily=...`

**ICAR disease knowledge API**
Does not exist publicly. No machine-readable API found.

**Soil data**
SoilGrids (ISRIC): `rest.isric.org/soilgrids/v2.0/properties/query` — free, CC-BY 4.0, global including India, 250m resolution, covers pH/N/OC/clay/sand/CEC. Rate limit: 5 calls/minute. Python package: `pip install soilgrids`. This is the best available free option for India.

---

## D. Vision LLM vs. Small Classifier — Recommendation

| Factor | llama3.2-vision:11b (Ollama) | EfficientNet-B3 fine-tuned |
|---|---|---|
| RAM usage | ~10GB in 4-bit (leaves 2GB for OS on 12GB) | ~300MB peak |
| Model size on disk | ~7.5GB | ~50MB |
| Inference speed (CPU) | 15–60 sec/image | 0.5–2 sec/image |
| Accuracy (in-domain) | Unknown for plant disease; general vision reasoning | 99%+ on PlantVillage lab images; 60–80% real-world |
| Offline | Yes (Ollama) | Yes (ONNX Runtime) |
| Extensibility | High — prompt it for new parameters (soil, market) | Low — needs retraining per new class |
| Pomegranate fine-tuning | Zero-shot, no fine-tuning needed | Requires fine-tuning on 5,099-image dataset |

**Practical recommendation for 12GB RAM Linux laptop: hybrid approach.**

Use a fine-tuned EfficientNet-B3 (or MobileNetV3) as the fast disease classifier — 50MB, ~1 sec inference, runs reliably with ONNX Runtime on CPU. Export the Roboflow pomegranate model to ONNX directly. Then use `llama3.2-vision:11b` via Ollama only as a secondary "explain and advise" layer: the classifier fires first (cheap, fast, accurate), and Llama gets the classifier result + weather/soil context to generate actionable advice in natural language. This avoids running 10GB Llama on every image while getting the extensibility of a general LLM for new parameters. With 12GB RAM, you can run Ollama in the background at 4-bit and keep ~2GB free for the OS and FastAPI server — tight but workable if you don't run the vision model concurrently with EfficientNet.

---

**Key starting files for this project:**
- Pomegranate dataset (2024, PMC): https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11220843/
- Roboflow pomegranate model: https://universe.roboflow.com/vehicle-detection-and-counting-fdrtm/pomegranate-disease-detection
- Best forkable FastAPI repo: https://github.com/pkparthk/Plant-Disease-Detection
- Open-Meteo docs: https://open-meteo.com/en/docs
- SoilGrids REST API: https://rest.isric.org/soilgrids/v2.0/properties/query
- SoilGrids Python library: https://github.com/gantian127/soilgrids

Sources:
- [PlantVillage Dataset - GitHub](https://github.com/spMohanty/PlantVillage-Dataset)
- [Comprehensive pomegranate fruit disease dataset (2024) - PMC](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11220843/)
- [Pomegranate Disease Detection - Roboflow Universe](https://universe.roboflow.com/vehicle-detection-and-counting-fdrtm/pomegranate-disease-detection)
- [wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit - HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/wambugu71/crop_leaf_diseases_vit)
- [prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection - HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/prof-freakenstein/plantnet-disease-detection)
- [Plant-Disease-Detection (FastAPI+React) - GitHub](https://github.com/pkparthk/Plant-Disease-Detection)
- [leaf-diseases-detect (FastAPI+LLaMA Vision) - GitHub](https://github.com/shukur-alom/leaf-diseases-detect)
- [PlantVillage Nuru - GitHub Org](https://github.com/PlantVillage)
- [Open-Meteo Free Weather API](https://open-meteo.com/)
- [Open-Meteo GitHub](https://github.com/open-meteo/open-meteo)
- [SoilGrids - ISRIC](https://isric.org/explore/soilgrids)
- [SoilGrids REST API](https://rest.isric.org/)
- [SoilGrids Python library - GitHub](https://github.com/gantian127/soilgrids)
- [llama3.2-vision - Ollama](https://ollama.com/library/llama3.2-vision)
- [Plant disease classification in the wild (ViT + MoE, 2025) - Frontiers](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1522985/full)
